You are a SEALED implementer under the barrier protocol at /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md — read it first and comply strictly.
ACCESS RULES: You must NOT read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/crates/, and NOT read any Haskell source of tamarin-prover anywhere on disk (notably /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/{lib,src}/ and /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/{lib,src}/). The ONLY Haskell you may read is the SANCTIONED BSD library /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/sanctioned/pretty-1.1.3.6/. Do NOT read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md, and do NOT read any AUDIT.md anywhere in the cleanroom (both-sides audit analysis lives there; any instruction you need from an audit is relayed below). No web access. You MAY read .spthy example files under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/examples/. No git commits.
DISCIPLINE: log every oracle interaction in workspace QUERIES.log (Session 10); record behavioral facts in BEHAVIOR.md with probe provenance; comments describe current behavior only. Oracle/server harness scripts carry OOM guards (oom_score_adj/ulimit) — preserve them; live HS servers only on ports 3200-3299 and STOP every server you start before returning. Return a PLAIN-TEXT summary.

Workspace: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/ (read/write freely inside it, except AUDIT.md). Your crate: workspace/graph-clean. Start by re-reading your own BEHAVIOR.md (§3f, §8, Round-9 report), QUERIES.log Session 9, and workspace/r9/ — round 10 continues directly from round 9's fill-width allocation model (two-layer trigger/fill, probe-exact 343/343 probe cells, corpus fill-band 90.94% of banded wrap cells).

ROUND-10 SCOPE, in priority order:

1. AUDIT REDO (behavioral instruction relayed from the round-9 review): your single-quoted-atom correction is logged inconsistently — QUERIES.log Session 9 and r9's fit2.py pin it as C(sqa)=flat−4 applied as a SIBLING-OCCUPANCY correction, while the shipped code and BEHAVIOR.md §3f apply −2 to the cell's OWN effective width with no occupancy correction. These predict different wrap boundaries on some shapes. Design live probes that distinguish the two placements/magnitudes, determine which matches the reference, and reconcile all three artifacts (log, spec, code) to the probed truth.

2. Probe-pin the function-node and multi-quote occupancy corrections you observed corpus-side in round 9 but could not pin (your note: P-series probe shapes were too narrow). Build wider batteries (vary sibling counts, function arities, quote multiplicities) and either pin exact corrections or record boundary-precise negative results.

3. Attack the band-NONE cell-doc gap (9.9% of wrap cells have NO engine width reproducing the observed bytes under your current cell-doc construction: ++-union terms, deep nesting, abbreviation-expansion layouts). Extend the cell-document model so these become bandable, probe-driven; even partial families closed (e.g. ++-unions alone) are wins. Record what remains with per-family counts.

4. INTERFACE EXTENSION for the largest residue family: 84% of your corpus fill misses involve abbreviation names because the reference decides widths on UN-abbreviATED internal renderings — structurally invisible to your crate, which consumes post-abbreviation text. Round 9 proposed the fix: extend the public entry points so a caller MAY supply per-cell occupancy values (and any other width inputs your model needs) that override your shape-feature estimates; behavior with the inputs absent must remain byte-identical to today (regression-gated). Specify the semantics precisely in BEHAVIOR.md and the interface notes so an adapter can be wired later; add tests exercising the supplied-occupancy path with synthetic values.

Acceptance: all existing tests stay green; GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS roundtrip stays 12022/12022; report fill_census before/after (all cells, wrapping cells, multi-cell wrapping, false-flat count) and the probe-battery hit rates. Do NOT edit anything outside /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/.

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing

Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.

## Rooms
- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
  as evidence of non-access.

## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
   boundary signatures only).
2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
   observation INPUTS.
3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.

## Forbidden to the clean room
- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.

## Audit & integration (after implementation)
1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
   forbidden path was accessed.
2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
3. Behavioral acceptance: the FULL-CORPUS gate for the slice must pass, not a
   fixture subset (see "Full-corpus gates" below). A narrow fixture suite that
   passes while the corpus gate fails is not acceptance.
4. Integration: the open side may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
   with them (history retains them under GPL). Every integrator that touches a
   slice with a full-corpus gate MUST run that gate before declaring green.

## Full-corpus gates (standard, not optional)

Each slice has a fast oracle gate over the whole 419-file example corpus,
diffing the slice's output against the Haskell reference cache. These are the
sealed implementer's primary data source (run between build and the HS cache
every iteration) AND the integrator's mandatory acceptance check. Both take
`RS_PATH` (point at any build) and `ALLOWLIST` (subset while iterating).

- Wellformedness: `scripts/wf_gate.sh` — runs the binary WITHOUT `--prove`
  (the wf block prints at theory-load, ~1s/file; full corpus ~72s at JOBS=6),
  extracts the `/* WARNING … */` block, diffs vs `scripts/.hs_file_cache`.
- Web: `scripts/web_parity.sh` — crawls HS and RS interactive servers per
  theory, semantic-diffs the response manifests vs `scripts/.web_hs_cache`.
- Full batch (proof output, slow ~30-60min): `scripts/corpus_file_diff.sh` —
  the ground-truth `--prove` byte diff; run at milestones, not every iteration.

Once a slice's ported code is DELETED, the integrated module IS the sealed
reimplementation, so running the gate against the tree observes the sealed
code's own output vs the reference — legitimate feedback, not observation of
replaced code. For a not-yet-integrated slice the tree still holds the ported
code, so gate only the workspace build (via `RS_PATH`), never the stock tree.

1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual: an unresolved *source* node feeding a present 161 premise) was **NOT observed** in any probe (Session-3 NAXOS/NSLPK3 case graphs, 162 Session-5 re-scan of NSLPK3 `cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` for i≤12,j≤8) nor anywhere 163 in the corpus/manifests. The invtrapezium is the *conclusion→absent-node* case 164 (its edge points **into** it: `n5:n2 -> n6`), i.e. the "conclusion feeding a node 165 absent from the graph"; the un-seen dual would be the mirror (`absent -> premise`). 166 Recorded unobserved; `GraphNode::Shaped{label,shape,color}` (generic) can emit any 167 shape a caller supplies. 168 169 ### 3g. Bare-timepoint ellipse + the `#last` designated timepoint (§ Round-5 item 3) 170 An uncolored ellipse whose label is a bare timepoint variable `#<var>`: 171 `n<ID>[label="#<var>",shape="ellipse"];`. Corpus census of ellipse labels found, 172 besides the `!KU(..)@#t` / `#t:rule[..]` / `Action(..)@#t` forms, bare-timepoint 173 nodes `#i` (1328), `#decrypt` (790), `#t0…#t6`, `#j`, `#j.1`, and the **designated 174 last timepoint `#last`** (107, e.g. `24a119958f784d43.dot`). `#last` arises when a 175 constraint system carries a last timepoint (induction / trace-property proofs); it 176 is the target of a `color="black",style="dashed"` before-edge. All render as the 177 plain uncolored ellipse above. Modelled as `GraphNode::Temporal{var}` (with 178 `GraphNode::last()` for `#last`). 179 180 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 181 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 182 id order. For each node in order: 183 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 184 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 185 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 186 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 187 188 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 189 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 190 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 191 192 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 193 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 194 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 195 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 196 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 197 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 198 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 199 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 200 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 201 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 202 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 203 against the corpus. 204 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 205 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 206 - **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are 207 separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented 208 with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element** 209 (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action 210 list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells. 211 - **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill, 212 width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first 213 line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat 214 width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories 215 (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and 216 sweeping the term width one column at a time: 217 - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat 218 rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Tested with functor names of length 219 2, 3, 6, 10 (`Ba`,`Out`,`Fact12`,`Factabcdef`): the boundary was **always** at 220 flat width 87 fits / 88 breaks, so the budget is counted from the functor's own 221 column 0, *including* the functor name (a longer name simply fits fewer args). 222 Corpus-consistent: 99.2 % of 190 044 physical lines have fact-content ≤ 87; the 223 residual are single unbreakable atoms wider than 87 (which overflow verbatim). 224 - **Greedy paragraph fill.** When a group overflows, elements pack left-to-right; 225 the separator `, ` trails the element it follows and stays on the line, and the 226 next element wraps when it would pass column 87. Continuation lines use the 227 §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 = 228 `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83), 229 `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line. 230 - **Delimiter peel — BYTE-IMPLEMENTED (Session 5).** Re-probed the boundary with 231 fresh single-node sweeps (`E10..E14`, `W69..W74`, ports 3210): a **tuple's `>`** 232 stays with the last element iff it fits (`Out( <'aaa(72)', 'y'>` │ `)`), else it 233 peels onto its own line at the tuple's **`<` column** (`W74`: `…'y'` │ `<5sp>>` 234 │ `)`); a **fact's `)` ALWAYS** peels onto its own line at **col 0** once the 235 fact wraps — even when it would fit (`E12`: `'a12'>` │ `)`) — because the padded 236 ` )` space is the break. An **info action-list `]`** stays attached to its last 237 action. An unbreakable atom wider than the budget overflows verbatim; only the 238 trailing delimiters wrap. 239 - **Continuation lookahead — RESOLVED (subsumed).** The old "first line 11, 240 continuation 12" claim is a mis-read: the continuation packs **greedily to the 241 same width 87** as the first line (`E13`→2, `E14`→3 elements on the second line, 242 each ending well under 87). There is no ±1 combinator lookahead to reproduce. 243 - **Info action-list = vertical `sep`, not fill.** An overflowing info cell puts 244 **one action per line** (corpus `6738eb64…`: 4 actions → 4 lines, short actions 245 included), `]` on the last; whereas a tuple / fact-arg list uses the greedy 246 fill. Two distinct combinators, both byte-implemented. 247 - **`graph-clean::render::wrap_cell`** implements all of the above (`layout_fact` 248 fill+`)`-peel, `layout_tuple` fill+`>`-peel, `layout_info` vertical `sep`, 249 `run_layout` greedy fill, `split_top_commas`), wired into 250 `generate::build_record`; byte-verified against the 7 captured probe fixtures 251 (`wrap_E11..E14`, `wrap_W71/W72/W74`). 252 - **The wrap TRIGGER is a per-GROUP shared budget — RESOLVED (Session 6).** The 253 old per-cell `flat > 87` decision is wrong for multi-cell groups. Each record 254 group (`premises`, `info`, `conclusions`) is laid out **independently** (a 255 conclusion cell's wrap ignores premise/info widths, and vice versa — live 256 probes PM/R: a lone conclusion `Out` of flat 87 stays flat with premises of 257 flat 69/103/198 and rule names of length 40). Within a group, the cells share 258 a budget: 259 > For a premise/conclusion group, let `T = Σ(flat width of every cell in the 260 > group)`. Cell *i* wraps **iff** `flat_i > budget_i`, where 261 > `budget_i = max(87 − (T − flat_i), 20)`. Equivalently: **cell *i* wraps iff 262 > `T > 87` AND `flat_i > 20`.** 263 Established by controlled live 2-/3-cell sweeps (QUERIES §6): with a fixed 264 second cell of flat *q*, the first cell's fit/wrap boundary is exactly 265 `flat = 87 − q` (q=11 → 76/77, q=28 → 58/61, q=48 → 39/40, q=68 → 19/22); a 266 third cell adds into the same Σ (3-cell [p,28,28] flips at `flat = 87−56 = 31`). 267 The **floor 20** is a per-cell minimum budget (a cell of flat ≤ 20 never wraps; 268 live: Fb flat 98 forces the sibling budget negative yet the sibling fits at 269 flat ≤ 20, wraps at 21). Corpus census: 0 non-wrapped 277 info cells carry a top-level action-list comma (all ≥2-action infos wrap). 278 - **Corpus validation at scale.** The rule matches actual `\l` on **99.635 %** of 279 776 259 cells and **98.324 %** of 160 409 records (info cells 99.968 %). Every 280 one of the 2 831 residual cells is within ~1–2 columns of its budget (mismatch 281 `T`-distribution clusters entirely at 79 ≤ T ≤ 95). At that exact boundary the 282 outcome is a **±1 HughesPJ `fits`/ribbon rounding artifact** (live: at a group 283 total of exactly `budget+1` the fit/wrap flips with atom parity; at `budget+5` 284 it wraps cleanly), depending on the cell's token structure, order, and the port 285 marker widths — reproducing it byte-exactly needs the GPL pretty-printer's 286 internal `fits`. The budget 289 **trigger** itself (does a cell wrap) is byte-exact away from the boundary. 290 - **The FILL width once a cell wraps ≠ the trigger budget — REFINED (Session 7).** 291 The wrap *trigger* is the flat-sum budget above (which cell breaks). The *fill* 292 width (how a broken cell packs) is **wider**: a sibling that itself wraps 293 occupies only the width it is ALLOCATED, not its flat width, so the remaining 294 cell packs more elements per line. Live datum (`Wide` conclusion group 295 `[Ack 25, Big 68, Out 11]`): the 68-flat `Big` cell packs **8** tuple elements 296 on line 0, as if its budget were **56** — not the flat-sum `87−(25+11) = 51` 297 (which packs 7). For 301 `Wide`: `Out`(11) fits; `Ack`, seen while `Big` is still at flat 68, is squeezed 302 to budget 20 and wraps (breaking after `~n.4`); `Big`, placed last, sees `Ack`'s 303 allocation 20 and gets `87 − 20 − 11 = 56` → 8 elements. Clustering / simplification (§ priority 3) 414 415 **Trigger rule (byte-verified over all 14705 graphs, 0 violations):** 416 > The COMPACT clustered rendering (§2b, with `subgraph "cluster_…"` blocks) is 417 > used **iff the graph contains at least one node with `role` ≠ `"Undefined"`.** 418 > Otherwise the SIMPLE header (§2a) is used. 419 420 Non-Undefined roles arise from process/SAPIC agents (e.g. `Initiator`, 421 `Responder`, `server`, `Tag`, `v`). Pure multiset-rewriting theories emit the 422 simple header everywhere; role-annotated theories emit the compact header on 423 every non-empty graph (empty graphs have no role nodes ⇒ simple). 424 425 ### Cluster block 426 ``` 427 subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_<k>" { 428 nodesep="0.6"; 429 ranksep="0.6"; 430 label="<Role>_Session_<k>"; 431 style="filled"; 432 color="#RRGGBBAA"; 433 penwidth="2"; 434 fillcolor="#RRGGBBAA"; 435 overlap="false"; 436 sep="4"; 437 n<ID>[shape="record",label="…",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="white",role="<Role>"]; 438 439 } 440 ``` 441 - Cluster name = `cluster_<clusterlabel>`; `label` = the clusterlabel 442 (`<Role>_Session_<k>`). Nodes grouped by (role, session instance). 443 - `color`/`fillcolor` are an 8-hex ARGB (alpha `4C`, ~30%) hashed from the 444 cluster; node `fillcolor` is the saturated per-role color (live SAPIC probe 445 `cluster_Process_Session_1` had node `fillcolor="#ffffff"`, `fontcolor="black"` 446 — so the node fill is not always saturated). Exact hash **[GAP]**. 447 448 ### Cluster EMISSION ORDER (§ Round-5 item 1) — byte-verified over 1457 corpus files 449 The top-level statement order of a clustered graph is **always** 450 `[free nodes] [clusters] [edges] [rankblock/legend] [invis edges]`: 0 files have a 451 top-level node after a cluster, 0 clusters after an edge. **Clusters contain only 452 records** (50 453 record cells, 0 ellipse/edge). A cluster's records occupy a 453 **contiguous** id range and clusters are ordered by increasing id (first-appearance 454 == id order). Since id order == emission order (§3e), the global id counter runs 455 over the free (non-role) nodes first — giving them the low ids — then over the 456 clustered records grouped by cluster. `graph-clean::generate` reproduces this: 457 Pass 1 allocates ids in `System.nodes` order, routing each role-record's statement 458 into its `cluster(label,color)` bucket and everything else to the free top level, 459 then emits free nodes, then cluster blocks in first-appearance order, then edges, 460 then the legend. Byte-exact on a live SAPIC single-cluster graph 461 (`cluster_process.dot`) and the multi-cluster corpus payload 462 `79c16911ad179d51.dot` (4 free ellipses + 1 cluster + 2 deduction edges). 463 464 ### What "simplification" means here 465 There is no exposed simplification *level*; the corpus reflects one fixed 466 rendering pipeline. The observable "simplification" is the fixed compression the 467 server applies before rendering: intruder-knowledge (`!KU`) nodes are drawn as 468 gray ellipses; single-premise/conclusion structural chains are drawn with bold 469 weight-10 edges; role nodes are packed into per-session clusters (compact mode). 470 Reproducing the *content* of that compression from a raw constraint system 471 requires the solver and is **[GAP]** — out of scope for a rendering crate. 472 473 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 474 ## 5. Node abbreviation (§ priority 2) 475 476 When a graph is drawn, sufficiently complex sub-terms are replaced by short 477 NAMES and listed in a legend node. Observed from 113 924 legend rows. 478 479 ### 5a. Legend node 480 A `plain` node with an HTML-like label, placed in a sink-rank block: 481 ``` 482 { 483 rank="sink"; 484 n<ID>[shape="plain",label=<<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1"><TR>…row0…</TR> 485 <65 spaces><TR>…row1…</TR> 486 … 487 <65 spaces><TR>…rowN…</TR></TABLE>>]; 488 489 } 490 ``` 491 - Each row: 492 `<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP"><FONT COLOR="#000000">NAME</FONT></TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">=</TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">EXPANSION</TD></TR>` 493 (single spaces between `</TD>` and the next `<TD>`). 494 - The first `<TR>` is inline after the `<TABLE …>` tag; every continuation `<TR>` 495 is on its own line indented by **65 spaces = the byte length of the 496 `<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1">` opening 497 tag** (graphviz hang-indent of the table's children; independent of node id). 498 - EXPANSION is HTML-escaped: `<`→`<`, `>`→`>`, `&`→`&`. Single quotes 499 and `* ^ ~ $ ⊕` are literal. 500 - The legend node is wired to the rest with `style="invis"` edges (from a couple 501 of real nodes to the legend), emitted AFTER the sink block. 502 503 ### 5b. Naming scheme (byte-verified rule) 504 `NAME = <PREFIX><n>`, n = 1,2,3,… per prefix. 505 `PREFIX` = the first **two alphabetic characters, uppercased**, of the ROOT 506 symbol's *name* (one letter if the name has only one). The root symbol name is: 507 - function application `f(…)` ⇒ `f` (`sign`→SI, `senc`→SE, `hash`→HA, 508 `h`/`h1`/`h2`→H, `KDF`/`kdf1`→KD, `pk`→PK, `aenc`/`aead`→AE, `pmult`→PM, …) 509 - public constant `'c'` ⇒ `c` (`'uninitialized'`→UN, `'F_status'`→FS, 510 `'ho_req_ack'`→HO, `'no_message_state'`→NO — non-letters skipped: F,s→FS) 511 - variable `~v` / `$v` / `v` ⇒ `v` (`~AMF_UE_NGAP_ID`→AM, `~cid_N26`→CI, 512 `~gNB_UE_ID`→GN, `~mid…`→MI, `commitmsg`→CO, `StateChannel`→ST) 513 - infix operator, mapped to its function name then the letter rule: 514 `^`→exp→**EX**, `*`→mult→**MU**, `++`→union→**UN**, `⊕`→xor→**XO**. 515 516 Abbreviations NEST: an inner abbreviated term is referenced by name inside an 517 outer expansion (`KD1='KDF(EX1)'`, `MA1='mac(EX1, SI1)'`, `EX1="'g'^MU1"`, 518 `H1="h(<…, EX2, EX3, EX1, …>)"`). So naming is applied bottom-up. 519 520 Per-prefix numbering is by tamarin's canonical term order, NOT by first 521 appearance in the drawing (observed: `In(EX2)` is the first occurrence yet gets 522 `EX2`, and larger/deeper terms get the lower numbers within a prefix). The exact 523 tie-break total order is **[GAP]** (needs the solver's term ordering); the crate 524 assigns per-prefix numbers over a defined order and matches whenever that order 525 is unambiguous. 526 527 ### 5c. Selection — WHICH sub-terms get abbreviated **[RESOLVED — see REPORT2.md]** 528 529 **Rule (three necessary gates; each confirmed by a controlled black-box probe):** 530 > A sub-term `t` is abbreviated **iff** 531 > 1. `renderLen(t) ≥ 10` (Unicode-scalar length of the fully-expanded surface 532 > rendering, *including* `'…'` quotes and `~`/`$` sigils), AND 533 > 2. `occ(t) ≥ 2` (occurrences over the whole constraint system — graph node 534 > facts **and** the sequent's goals/formulas — edge-shared messages count 535 > once), AND 536 > 3. `t` is **not** a tuple `<…>`. 537 > Naming is bottom-up: nested eligible sub-terms are named first 538 > (`LO1='longarg123'`, then `H1='h(LO1)'`). 539 540 This is a rendered **length** gate, not the *size* or *occurrence* the earlier 541 note guessed at — which is why it looked context-free-yet-unexplained. It 542 cleanly settles the old counter-observations: `sign(<…,'g'^~ekR>,~ltkI)` is ≥10 543 chars (abbreviated); `KDF(z)` is 6 chars (never abbreviated, whatever its count); 544 `mac(z,SI1)` and `'g'^~lkR` are below the length/occurrence thresholds in their 545 graphs. `'g'^~lkR` vs `'g'^~lkR.1` differ because the `.1` copy reaches occ 2 in 546 the system while the other does not. 547 548 **Corpus-exactness (necessary direction):** across all **97 538** legend 549 expansions in **11 564** graphs, the minimum rendered length is exactly **10** 550 (0 below) and **0** entries are top-level tuples. Among terms drawn ≥2× in a 551 graph, **0** abbreviated terms violate len≥10 ∧ ¬tuple. Abbreviated terms with 552 graph-occurrence 1 (7 800) all reach ≥2 once the sequent is counted. 553 554 **Controlled probes (crafted theories, live server; see QUERIES.log):** 555 - `'12345678'` (10 chars, ×2) abbreviated; `'1234567'` (9, ×2) not ⇒ boundary = 10. 556 - `'ABCDEFGH'`/`'onceonly12'` (×1) not abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (×2) is; 557 a 42-char constant (×1) not abbreviated ⇒ occ ≥ 2 is a hard gate at any length. 558 - `<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, ×2) not abbreviated while sibling `h('longarg123')` 559 (×2) is ⇒ tuple exclusion + bottom-up nesting. 560 561 **Residual (sufficiency):** for **AC/DH-operator** sub-terms (`^ * ⊕ ++ inv em 562 hp pmult`) tamarin counts occurrences over the *normalised* (AC-flattened, 563 DH-normal-form) term, not the surface rendering, so the same term can be 564 abbreviated in one proof state and not another (`em(hp($A),hp($B))` inline in 565 state `56eeb42d`, `EM1` in `54eb2e2c`). 93 % of apparent exceptions are such 566 AC/DH cases; the rest are validation artifacts from expanding shared 567 abbreviations before counting. Outside AC/DH terms the rule is exact. 568 569 The crate `abbrev::select` implements this rule (`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, 570 `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2`, tuple-excluded, bottom-up); tests reproduce every probe. 571 Byte-parity of the selection set is claimed for non-AC/DH terms and gated by the 572 caller supplying the full-system term multiset. 573 574 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 575 ## 6. System → graph mapping (§ Round-3 item 4) 576 577 From paired `main/proof` sequents and `interactive-graph-def` graphs, the 578 structural mapping a proof state yields (each point traces to §3d/§3f and the 579 content census; the *content* — which nodes/edges exist — is a solver GAP): 580 - a **rule instance** `#t : Rule[actions]` → a `record` node with groups 581 `{premises}|{info}|{conclusions}` (empty prem/concl dropped, info always kept); 582 - an **intruder-knowledge fact** → a gray `!KU( m ) @ #t` ellipse; 583 - a **protocol action/event** → a darkblue `Fact @ #t` ellipse; 584 - a **compressed intruder rule** → an uncolored `#t : rule` ellipse (§4 compression); 585 - an **unresolved node** referenced by an open premise → an `invtrapezium` 586 `(#var, idx)` (§3d); 587 - **edges** connect conclusion-port → premise-port (structural bold w10 [gray50]), 588 intruder deduction (red dashed), message (gray30), temporal order 589 (blue3/black dashed), etc. — the finite §3c vocabulary; 590 - ids from §3e; header from §4 (role); legend from §5. 591 592 `graph-clean::generate` implements this over an independent input model 593 (`System`/`GraphNode`/`RuleInstance`/`SysEdge`), taking node/edge lists, colors, 594 and cell text as INPUTS. `tests/generate_tests.rs` reproduces a live source-case 595 graph (`nsl_invtrap.dot`) **byte-exact** end-to-end. 596 597 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 598 ## 7. Simplification / abbreviation options (§ Round-3 item 5) 599 600 The UI's "Graph simplification off/L1/L2/L3" menu and abbreviation toggle are 601 cookie-driven and appended by the JS as graph-URL **query params** (mined from 602 `/static/js/tamarin-prover-ui.js`, confirmed by live diffs — QUERIES.log): 603 604 | param | UI sends when | observed effect | 605 |--------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------| 606 | `simplification=N` | always (N=level, default 2) | **inert** — the *number* changes nothing (see below) | 607 | `uncompact=` | level 0 only | node COMPACTION off (lone intruder rule → full record vs ellipse) | 608 | `uncompress=` | level 0 only | system COMPRESSION off (Fresh sources + intruder rules re-expand to records) | 609 | `unabbreviate=` | abbreviate cookie off | abbreviation off (terms inlined, legend block omitted) | 610 | `no-auto-sources=` | auto-sources cookie off | disables auto-source precomputation | 611 | `clustering=true` | clustering cookie on | forces role clustering | 612 613 ### 7a. The L1/L2/L3 distinction — RESOLVED (channel-verified negative, Session 4) 614 615 The Round-3 GAP ("simplification 1/2/3 produced byte-identical graphs") is **not a 616 probing artifact and not a hidden session channel** — it is the genuine server 617 behavior: **there is no L1/L2/L3 distinction; the `simplification=N` *number* is 618 inert for every N.** Established under the session-state lens the follow-up 619 demanded: 620 621 - **Channel.** The served JS (`tamarin-prover-ui.js`, lines 78–100 / 476–500) 622 reads a *client-side* `simplification` cookie (default 2) and converts it into 623 the graph-URL query param; at level 0 it *additionally* appends `uncompact=` + 624 `uncompress=`. Levels 1/2/3 send **only** `simplification=N`. The server emits 625 **no `Set-Cookie` on any response** (root, case-graph, main/cases json, proof 626 method) — there is *no server-side session state*; the param is the only channel. 627 - **Change verified before diffing.** Driving the *flags* through the URL param 628 demonstrably changes the graph (e.g. rich NSLPK3 case `refined/6/5`: default 629 5 668 B → `uncompact=&uncompress=` 7 792 B), and the result is byte-identical 630 whether or not a matching cookie is also sent. So the URL param **is** honored. 631 - **The number is inert.** On that 17-node case graph and on a **39-node, 27 KB** 632 DH/bilinear proof-state graph (`eurosp19-eccDAA` analysed, 633 `proof/functional_correctness/_`), every probe of `simplification` ∈ 634 {0,1,2,3,4,5,10,99} — via URL param, via cookie, and via both together, and 635 crossed with every flag combination — collapses to a **single** md5. `flags_L0` 636 … `flags_L99` are byte-identical. (Second theory NAXOS_eCK case `refined/4/1` 637 reproduces this.) 638 - **Mechanism.** The UI's four-item menu (`for i<4`: off / L1 / L2 / L3) dresses up 639 a *binary*: "off" (level 0) = the UI adds the two flags; "L1/L2/L3" = no flags, 640 and the server never consumes the level integer. The real transforms are the 641 independent boolean flags, each with a distinct, measured effect on the 39-node 642 graph: `uncompact=` 39 nodes→richer records (27 361→30 880 B); `uncompress=` 643 39→**74** nodes (re-expands compressed chains, 31 407 B); `unabbreviate=` drops 644 the legend node (39→38) and inlines terms (36 690 B, 0 legend rows); 645 level 0 (`uncompact`+`uncompress`) = 74 nodes, byte-identical to the two flags 646 sent explicitly. 647 648 `graph-clean::options::Options` models the knobs and reproduces the exact query 649 string; `simplification=N` is carried but is documented inert. The abbreviation 650 on/off transform is applied directly (drop legend / inline). Remaining **GAP** 651 here: the *content* of compress/compact (a solver transform — the caller supplies 652 the already-(un)compressed node set). 653 654 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 655 ## 8. 422	    at T=90; (39,41) sib wraps at T=80) + L/M edges pin SHAPE-CORRECTED
423	    occupancies C: C(argfact)=flat, C(single-quoted-atom-fact)=flat-4,
424	    C(tuple-fact)=flat+2n-4 (n = tuple elems); trigger b_i = max(87 - sum_{j!=i}
425	    C_j, 20); every probe edge (39,36)/(55,36)/(71,19)/(71,21)/M_26/M_28/J_43/

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Fit trigger + fill layers with shape-corrected occupancies C.
3	
4	bands2 format per cell: flat:STATUS:dtop:drec:nq:sqa:nargs
5	Trigger model: cell wraps iff flat > max(W - sum_{j!=i} C_j, FLOOR)
6	  C_j = flat_j + at*dtop_j (or drec) + aq*nq_j + asq*sqa_j
7	Fill model candidates for wrapping cells (budget in ribbon space -> L=3b//2):
8	  resid   = trigger budget
9	  propF   = round-family(W * flat_i / sum flat_j)
10	  propC   = W * C_i-ish / sum C_j ...
11	  maxRP   = max(resid, propF)
12	"""
13	import sys, math
14	from collections import Counter
15	
16	def rib_to_L(b):
17	    return (3 * int(b)) // 2
18	
19	def parse(path):
20	    groups = []
21	    with open(path) as f:
22	        for line in f:
23	            p = line.rstrip("\n").split("\t")
24	            if len(p) != 6:
25	                continue
26	            cells = []
27	            okrow = True
28	            for c in p[5].split("|"):
29	                seg = c.split(":")
30	                if len(seg) != 7:
31	                    okrow = False
32	                    break
33	                flat = int(seg[0]); status = seg[1]
34	                dtop, drec, nq, sqa, nargs = map(int, seg[2:])
35	                if status.startswith("F"):
36	                    lo = None if status in ("FNONE",) else int(status[1:])
37	                    cells.append(dict(f=flat, kind="fit", lo=lo, dtop=dtop,
38	                                      drec=drec, nq=nq, sqa=sqa, na=nargs))
39	                elif status == "NONE":
40	                    cells.append(dict(f=flat, kind="wrap", runs=None, dtop=dtop,
41	                                      drec=drec, nq=nq, sqa=sqa, na=nargs))
42	                else:
43	                    runs = [tuple(map(int, r.split("-"))) for r in status.split(",")]
44	                    cells.append(dict(f=flat, kind="wrap", runs=runs, dtop=dtop,
45	                                      drec=drec, nq=nq, sqa=sqa, na=nargs))
46	            if okrow and cells:
47	                groups.append(dict(file=p[0], kind=p[1], cells=cells))
48	    return groups
49	
50	def C_of(c, at, aq, asq, use_rec=False):
51	    d = c["drec"] if use_rec else c["dtop"]
52	    return c["f"] + at * d + aq * c["nq"] + asq * c["sqa"]
53	
54	def trigger_eval(groups, at, aq, asq, W=87, floor=20, use_rec=False):
55	    """Return (wrong_wrap_pred, wrong_fit_pred, total)."""
56	    fp = fn = tot = 0
57	    for g in groups:
58	        cs = g["cells"]
59	        if len(cs) < 2:
60	            continue
61	        Cs = [C_of(c, at, aq, asq, use_rec) for c in cs]
62	        S = sum(Cs)
63	        for c, Ci in zip(cs, Cs):
64	            b = max(W - (S - Ci), floor)
65	            pred_wrap = c["f"] > b
66	            obs_wrap = c["kind"] == "wrap"
67	            tot += 1
68	            if pred_wrap and not obs_wrap:
69	                fp += 1
70	            elif not pred_wrap and obs_wrap:
71	                fn += 1
72	    return fp, fn, tot
73	
74	def in_band(c, L):
75	    if c["kind"] == "fit":
76	        return c["lo"] is not None and L >= c["lo"]
77	    if c["runs"] is None:
78	        return False
79	    return any(a <= L <= b for a, b in c["runs"])
80	
81	def fill_eval(groups, fill_fn, at, aq, asq, W=87, floor=20, use_rec=False):
82	    """fill_fn(flats, Cs, i, trig_b) -> ribbon budget for wrapping cell i."""
83	    hit = tot = none = fitviol = fittot = 0
84	    for g in groups:
85	        cs = g["cells"]
86	        if len(cs) < 2:
87	            continue
88	        Cs = [C_of(c, at, aq, asq, use_rec) for c in cs]
89	        S = sum(Cs)
90	        flats = [c["f"] for c in cs]
91	        for i, c in enumerate(cs):
92	            trig_b = max(87 - (S - Cs[i]), floor)
93	            b = fill_fn(flats, Cs, i, trig_b)
94	            L = rib_to_L(b)
95	            if c["kind"] == "wrap":
96	                if c["runs"] is None:
97	                    none += 1
98	                    continue
99	                tot += 1
100	                if in_band(c, L):
101	                    hit += 1
102	            else:
103	                fittot += 1
104	                if not in_band(c, L):
105	                    fitviol += 1
106	    return hit, tot, none, fitviol, fittot
107	
108	def main():
109	    paths = sys.argv[1:]
110	    groups = []
111	    for p in paths:
112	        groups += parse(p)
113	    multi = [g for g in groups if len(g["cells"]) >= 2]
114	    print(f"multi-cell groups: {len(multi)}")
115	
116	    print("== trigger grid (fp = pred wrap, obs fit; fn = pred fit, obs wrap)")
117	    for (at, aq, asq, rec) in [
118	        (0, 0, 0, False),        # plain flat-sum
119	        (2, 0, -4, False),       # tuple 2n-4 top-level, sqa -4
120	        (2, 0, -4, True),        # recursive tuples
121	        (2, -2, 0, False),       # per-quote -2 instead of sqa
122	        (2, -2, -2, False),
123	        (2, 0, -2, False),
124	        (1, 0, -4, False),
125	        (3, 0, -4, False),
126	        (2, -1, -3, False),
127	    ]:
128	        fp, fn, tot = trigger_eval(multi, at, aq, asq, use_rec=rec)
129	        print(f"  at={at} aq={aq} asq={asq} rec={int(rec)}: fp {fp}  fn {fn}  err {100*(fp+fn)/max(tot,1):.3f}% of {tot}")
130	
131	    print("== fill candidates (band-hit on wrapping cells; trigger at=2,asq=-4)")
132	    at, aq, asq = 2, 0, -4
133	    def resid(fl, Cs, i, tb):
134	        return tb
135	    def propF(fl, Cs, i, tb):
136	        T = sum(fl)
137	        return max(int(math.floor(87 * fl[i] / T)), 20)
138	    def propF_half(fl, Cs, i, tb):
139	        T = sum(fl)
140	        return max(int(math.floor(87 * fl[i] / T + 0.5)), 20)
141	    def propC(fl, Cs, i, tb):
142	        T = sum(Cs)
143	        return max(int(math.floor(87 * Cs[i] / T + 0.5)), 20)
144	    def maxRP(fl, Cs, i, tb):
145	        return max(tb, propF_half(fl, Cs, i, tb))
146	    def maxRPf(fl, Cs, i, tb):
147	        return max(tb, propF(fl, Cs, i, tb))
148	    def propFsibC(fl, Cs, i, tb):
149	        T = fl[i] + sum(Cs) - Cs[i]
150	        return max(int(math.floor(87 * fl[i] / T + 0.5)), 20)
151	    def maxR_propFsibC(fl, Cs, i, tb):
152	        return max(tb, propFsibC(fl, Cs, i, tb))
153	    for name, fn_ in [("resid", resid), ("propF_floor", propF),
154	                      ("propF_half", propF_half), ("propC", propC),
155	                      ("max(resid,propF_half)", maxRP),
156	                      ("max(resid,propF_floor)", maxRPf),
157	                      ("propF_sibC", propFsibC),
158	                      ("max(resid,propF_sibC)", maxR_propFsibC)]:
159	        hit, tot, none, fv, ft = fill_eval(multi, fn_, at, aq, asq)
160	        print(f"  {name:24s}: band-hit {hit}/{tot} = {100*hit/max(tot,1):.2f}%  (NONE {none})  fit-viol {fv}/{ft}")
161	
162	if __name__ == "__main__":
163	    main()
164

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	# Round-9 probe battery #2: pin per-cell budgets to +-1 column.
3	# I: [Big(<16 $vars>)=87, Sib('a'*p+id)] with sib flat s stepping by 1 col.
4	# J: equal-pair trigger fine sweep (a,a), a = 41..46 (argfacts).
5	# K: triple trigger fine sweep (a,a,a), a = 28..32.
6	# L: mixed breakable/unbreakable pair at T ~= 84..90, both orders.
7	# M: 2-cell argfact pairs, second cell stepped by 1 col (budget of first).
8	import sys
9	
10	def pvar(i):
11	    a = "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
12	    return "$" + a[i//26] + a[i%26]
13	
14	def pvars(n, off=0):
15	    return [pvar(i+off) for i in range(n)]
16	
17	def bigfact(name, n, off=0):
18	    return f"{name}(<{', '.join(pvars(n, off))}>)"
19	
20	def argfact(name, n, off=0):
21	    return f"{name}({', '.join(pvars(n, off))})"
22	
23	def sib(p, idx, name="Sib"):
24	    return f"{name}('{'a'*p}{idx}')"
25	
26	def in_prem(k, off=100):
27	    return f"In(<{', '.join(pvars(k, off))}>)"
28	
29	rules, lemmas, combos = [], [], []
30	n = 0
31	
32	def emit(name, prems, concls, comment):
33	    global n
34	    rules.append(
35	        f"// {comment}\n"
36	        f"rule {name}:\n  [ {', '.join(prems)} ]\n  --[ F{n}() ]->\n  [ {', '.join(concls)} ]\n")
37	    lemmas.append(f"lemma l_{name}:\n  exists-trace \"Ex #i. F{n}() @ #i\"\n")
38	    combos.append(name)
39	    n += 1
40	
41	# --- Series I: Big 87 fixed; sib flat s = p+11, s stepping by 1 within windows
42	for s in [21,22,23,24, 26, 31,32,33,34, 36, 41,42,43,44, 46, 51,52,53,54,
43	          61,62,63,64, 71,72,73,74]:
44	    p = s - 11
45	    emit(f"I_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
46	         [bigfact("Big", 16), sib(p, f"{n:02d}")],
47	         f"I: concl [Big 87, Sib {s}]")
48	
49	# --- Series J: equal argfact pairs, T fine sweep around 87
50	# argfact flat = len(name)+5n+2. Use name lengths to hit odd widths.
51	# (name,nargs) -> flat: Faa+8 -> 45; Fa+8 -> 44; F+8 -> 43; Faaa+8 -> 46; F+8?
52	Jspecs = [("F", 8, 43), ("Fa", 8, 44), ("Faa", 8, 45), ("Faaa", 8, 46),
53	          ("Fh", 7, 39), ("Faaaa", 8, 47)]
54	for (nm, k, flat) in Jspecs:
55	    nm2 = "G" + nm[1:] if False else nm.replace("F", "H", 1)
56	    emit(f"J_{flat:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
57	         [argfact(nm, k), argfact(nm2 if nm2 != nm else nm+"x", k, off=k)],
58	         f"J: concl pair [{flat}, {flat}]")
59	
60	# --- Series K: equal triples around T=87..96
61	Kspecs = [("F", 5, 28), ("Fa", 5, 29), ("Faa", 5, 30), ("Faaa", 5, 31), ("Faaaa", 5, 32)]
62	for (nm, k, flat) in Kspecs:
63	    emit(f"K_{flat:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
64	         [argfact(nm, k), argfact(nm.replace("F", "H", 1), k, off=k),
65	          argfact(nm.replace("F", "J", 1), k, off=2*k)],
66	         f"K: concl triple [{flat} x3]")
67	
68	# --- Series L: breakable Faa(8)=45 with unbreakable Sib s, both orders, T 84..92
69	for s in [39, 41, 43, 45, 47]:
70	    p = s - 11
71	    emit(f"LA_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
72	         [argfact("Faa", 8), sib(p, f"{n:02d}")],
73	         f"LA: concl [Faa 45, Sib {s}]")
74	    emit(f"LB_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
75	         [sib(p, f"{n:02d}"), argfact("Faa", 8)],
76	         f"LB: concl [Sib {s}, Faa 45]")
77	
78	# --- Series M: [Wide argfact 60, Nar argfact stepped by 1] -> budget of Wide
79	# Wide = Faa 11 args = 60. Nar: name len varies: F+4=23,Fa+4=24,Faa+4=25,Faaa+4=26,
80	# and 5-arg versions 28..31
81	Mspecs = [("F",4,23),("Fa",4,24),("Faa",4,25),("Faaa",4,26),
82	          ("F",5,28),("Fa",5,29),("Faa",5,30),("Faaa",5,31)]
83	for (nm, k, flat) in Mspecs:
84	    emit(f"M_{flat:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
85	         [argfact("Wide", 11), argfact(nm.replace("F","N",1), k, off=11)],
86	         f"M: concl [Wide 62, Nar {flat}]")
87	
88	with open("probe3.spthy", "w") as f:
89	    f.write("theory BudgetPinProbe\nbegin\n\nbuiltins: hashing\n\n")
90	    for r in rules:
91	        f.write(r + "\n")
92	    for l in lemmas:
93	        f.write(l + "\n")
94	    f.write("end\n")
95	with open("probe3.names", "w") as f:
96	    for c in combos:
97	        f.write(c + "\n")
98	print(f"wrote probe3.spthy with {len(rules)} rules")
99

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	# Autoprove each lemma of a served theory and fetch its proof-state graph DOT.
3	# Usage: drive.py <port> <lemma1> [lemma2 ...]   -> prints "LEMMA\t<dotpath>" lines
4	import sys, re, json, urllib.request, os
5	PORT=sys.argv[1]; LEMMAS=sys.argv[2:]
6	BASE=f"http://127.0.0.1:{PORT}"
7	OUT=os.environ.get("OUTDIR","/tmp/probe_dots"); os.makedirs(OUT,exist_ok=True)
8	def get(path, timeout=300):
9	    with urllib.request.urlopen(BASE+path, timeout=timeout) as r:
10	        return r.read().decode("utf-8","replace")
11	def latest_index():
12	    idx=get("/")
13	    ms=re.findall(r'/thy/trace/(\d+)/', idx)
14	    return max(int(x) for x in ms) if ms else 1
15	for L in LEMMAS:
16	    i=latest_index()
17	    # autoprove this lemma from the current latest theory
18	    try:
19	        r=get(f"/thy/trace/{i}/autoprove/idfs/0/False/proof/{L}")
20	    except Exception as e:
21	        print(f"{L}\tAUTOPROVE_ERR {e}"); continue
22	    # response is JSON with a redirect to the new theory index
23	    ni=i
24	    m=re.search(r'/thy/trace/(\d+)/', r)
25	    if m: ni=int(m.group(1))
26	    try:
27	        dot=get(f"/thy/trace/{ni}/interactive-graph-def/proof/{L}/_")
28	    except Exception as e:
29	        print(f"{L}\tGRAPH_ERR {e}"); continue
30	    p=os.path.join(OUT,f"{L}.dot")
31	    open(p,"w").write(dot)
32	    print(f"{L}\t{p}\t{len(dot)}")

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# OOM guard: prover is the preferred kill target, capped at 24GB address space
echo 1000 > /proc/self/oom_score_adj 2>/dev/null || true
ulimit -v 25165824 2>/dev/null || true
# Black-box oracle: serve a .spthy in the HS interactive web UI.
# Usage: hs_server.sh start <file.spthy> <port>   (then curl http://127.0.0.1:<port>/...)
#        hs_server.sh stop <port>
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
case "$1" in
  start) d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"; nohup "/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover" interactive "$d" --port "$3" >/tmp/hs_server_$3.log 2>&1 & echo $! > /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid; sleep 4; echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid))";;
  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
esac

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Regress pinned (narrow-band) cell budgets against group-composition features.
3	
4	A pinned cell: wrapping, band width in ribbon space <= MAXW. Its observed budget
5	b_obs ~ band midpoint. Features: f, T, n, sum_others, sum_big_others,
6	n_small (flats<=20 among others), kind, info.
7	Least squares by hand (no numpy): solve normal equations via Gaussian elim.
8	"""
9	import sys, math
10	from collections import defaultdict
11	from fit import parse
12	
13	def rib(L):
14	    v = L / 1.5
15	    fl = math.floor(v)
16	    if abs(v - fl - 0.5) < 1e-9:
17	        return fl if fl % 2 == 0 else fl + 1
18	    return round(v)
19	
20	def gather(groups, maxw=2):
21	    rows = []
22	    for g in groups:
23	        cells = g["cells"]
24	        if len(cells) < 2:
25	            continue
26	        flats = [c[0] for c in cells]
27	        T = sum(flats)
28	        n = len(flats)
29	        for i, c in enumerate(cells):
30	            if c[1] != "wrap" or c[2] is None:
31	                continue
32	            lo, hi = c[2][0][0], c[2][-1][1]
33	            rlo, rhi = rib(lo), rib(hi)
34	            if rhi - rlo > maxw:
35	                continue
36	            others = [flats[j] for j in range(n) if j != i]
37	            rows.append({
38	                "f": flats[i], "T": T, "n": n,
39	                "so": sum(others),
40	                "sbig": sum(x for x in others if x > 20),
41	                "nsmall": sum(1 for x in others if x <= 20),
42	                "nwrapo": sum(1 for j in range(n) if j != i and cells[j][1] == "wrap"),
43	                "b": (rlo + rhi) / 2, "rlo": rlo, "rhi": rhi,
44	                "kind": g["kind"], "info": g["info"], "file": g["file"],
45	                "flats": flats, "i": i,
46	            })
47	    return rows
48	
49	def lstsq(X, y):
50	    k = len(X[0])
51	    A = [[sum(X[r][a] * X[r][b] for r in range(len(X))) for b in range(k)] for a in range(k)]
52	    v = [sum(X[r][a] * y[r] for r in range(len(X))) for a in range(k)]
53	    for c in range(k):
54	        p = max(range(c, k), key=lambda r: abs(A[r][c]))
55	        A[c], A[p] = A[p], A[c]
56	        v[c], v[p] = v[p], v[c]
57	        for r in range(c + 1, k):
58	            m = A[r][c] / A[c][c]
59	            for cc in range(c, k):
60	                A[r][cc] -= m * A[c][cc]
61	            v[r] -= m * v[c]
62	    beta = [0.0] * k
63	    for r in range(k - 1, -1, -1):
64	        beta[r] = (v[r] - sum(A[r][c] * beta[c] for c in range(r + 1, k))) / A[r][r]
65	    return beta
66	
67	if __name__ == "__main__":
68	    groups = parse(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "bands.tsv")
69	    rows = gather(groups, maxw=2)
70	    # dedupe identical (flats, i, band) observations to avoid corpus duplication bias
71	    seen = {}
72	    for r in rows:
73	        key = (tuple(r["flats"]), r["i"], r["rlo"], r["rhi"])
74	        seen.setdefault(key, r)
75	    rows = list(seen.values())
76	    print(f"pinned unique cells: {len(rows)}")
77	    # global linear fit b ~ a*f + b*sbig + c*nsmall + d*n + e
78	    X = [[r["f"], r["sbig"], r["nsmall"], r["n"], 1.0] for r in rows]
79	    y = [r["b"] for r in rows]
80	    beta = lstsq(X, y)
81	    pred = [sum(x * b for x, b in zip(row, beta)) for row in X]
82	    inband = sum(1 for r, p in zip(rows, pred) if r["rlo"] <= p <= r["rhi"])
83	    resid = [p - yy for p, yy in zip(pred, y)]
84	    rmse = math.sqrt(sum(e * e for e in resid) / len(resid))
85	    print("beta [f, sbig, nsmall, n, 1] =", [round(b, 4) for b in beta])
86	    print(f"rmse {rmse:.2f}; in-band {inband}/{len(rows)}")
87	    # fit separately by n
88	    byn = defaultdict(list)
89	    for r in rows:
90	        byn[r["n"]].append(r)
91	    for n in sorted(byn):
92	        rs = byn[n]
93	        if len(rs) < 12:
94	            continue
95	        X = [[r["f"], r["sbig"], r["nsmall"], 1.0] for r in rs]
96	        y = [r["b"] for r in rs]
97	        beta = lstsq(X, y)
98	        pred = [sum(x * b for x, b in zip(row, beta)) for row in X]
99	        inb = sum(1 for r, p in zip(rs, pred) if r["rlo"] <= p <= r["rhi"])
100	        rmse = math.sqrt(sum((p - yy) ** 2 for p, yy in zip(pred, y)) / len(rs))
101	        print(f"n={n}: {len(rs)} cells, beta[f,sbig,nsmall,1]={[round(b,3) for b in beta]}, rmse {rmse:.2f}, in-band {inb}/{len(rs)}")
102	    # dump n=2 pinned pairs for eyeballing
103	    print("\nn=2 pinned cells (f, sibling, b rlo-rhi):")
104	    n2 = sorted([r for r in rows if r["n"] == 2], key=lambda r: (r["f"], r["so"]))
105	    for r in n2[:80]:
106	        print(f"  f={r['f']:4d} sib={r['so']:4d} b={r['rlo']}-{r['rhi']} file={r['file']} i={r['i']}")

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Analyze prop(87) misses against per-cell L-bands; look for structure."""
3	import sys, math
4	from collections import Counter, defaultdict
5	from fit import parse, rib_to_L, ok, alloc_prop
6	
7	groups = parse(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "bands.tsv")
8	multi = [g for g in groups if len(g["cells"]) >= 2]
9	
10	# ribbon-space band: for L in band runs, ribbon = banker's round(L/1.5)
11	def rib(L):
12	    v = L / 1.5
13	    fl = math.floor(v)
14	    if abs(v - fl - 0.5) < 1e-9:
15	        return fl if fl % 2 == 0 else fl + 1
16	    return round(v)
17	
18	miss_dir = Counter()
19	by_n = defaultdict(lambda: [0, 0])
20	by_kind = defaultdict(lambda: [0, 0])
21	deltas = Counter()
22	miss_examples = []
23	# Correlate miss direction with composition features
24	feat = defaultdict(lambda: [0, 0])
25	
26	for g in multi:
27	    flats = [c[0] for c in g["cells"]]
28	    T = sum(flats)
29	    n = len(flats)
30	    budgets = alloc_prop(flats)
31	    Ls = [rib_to_L(b) for b in budgets]
32	    nwrapping = sum(1 for c in g["cells"] if c[1] == "wrap")
33	    for i, (c, L, b) in enumerate(zip(g["cells"], Ls, budgets)):
34	        if c[1] != "wrap" or c[2] is None:
35	            continue
36	        hit = ok(c, L)
37	        by_n[n][0 if hit else 1] += 1
38	        by_kind[g["kind"]][0 if hit else 1] += 1
39	        if not hit:
40	            lo, hi = c[2][0][0], c[2][-1][1]
41	            # signed distance in ribbon space
42	            rlo, rhi = rib(lo), rib(hi)
43	            if L < lo:
44	                d = rlo - b
45	                miss_dir["alloc-too-small"] += 1
46	            elif L > hi:
47	                d = rhi - b
48	                miss_dir["alloc-too-big"] += 1
49	            else:
50	                d = 0
51	                miss_dir["in-gap-between-runs"] += 1
52	            deltas[max(-30, min(30, d))] += 1
53	            # features
54	            other = T - flats[i]
55	            feat[("wrapsibs>0", any(g["cells"][j][1] == "wrap" for j in range(n) if j != i))][0 if hit else 1] += 1
56	            if len(miss_examples) < 40 and d != 0:
57	                miss_examples.append((g["file"], g["kind"], flats, i, b, (rlo, rhi), d, g["info"]))
58	        else:
59	            feat[("wrapsibs>0", any(g["cells"][j][1] == "wrap" for j in range(n) if j != i))][0] += 1
60	
61	print("miss directions:", dict(miss_dir))
62	print("by n cells (hit, miss):")
63	for n in sorted(by_n):
64	    h, m = by_n[n]
65	    print(f"  n={n}: {h}/{h+m} = {100*h/(h+m):.1f}%")
66	print("by group kind:", {k: (v[0], v[1]) for k, v in by_kind.items()})
67	print("delta (needed_ribbon - allocated_ribbon) histogram (capped +-30):")
68	for d in sorted(deltas):
69	    print(f"  {d:+3d}: {deltas[d]}")
70	print("\nmiss examples (file kind flats idx alloc_b band_rib delta info):")
71	for e in miss_examples[:30]:
72	    print("  ", e)

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# Round-10 probe generators. Four batteries, one .spthy each:
#   probeA.spthy (AuditSqaProbe)  — task 1: distinguish the two logged sqa models.
#     RA: [Faa(8 $vars)=45, Sib(s) sqa] s=40..48 step 1 (fills r9 parity gaps).
#         X wraps iff s > 42+k (k = sqa occupancy correction, C=flat-k);
#         Sib wraps iff s > 42+d (d = sqa own-width discount, eff=flat-d).
#     RB: [Faaa(8)=46, Sib(s)] s=39..46 (replication at a second base).
#     RC: order swap [Sib(s), Faa 45] s in {42,44}.
#     RD: triple [F(5)=28, H(5)=28, Sib(s)] s=30..35 (k,d in a 3-cell row).
#     RE: floor [Wf(13)=70, Sib(s)] s=20..24 (discount at the floor-20 budget).
#   probeB.spthy (FuncShapeProbe) — task 2: function-node corrections.
#     FB: [Faa 45, Q(func(...)) s] s=38..48 (func occupancy via X's flip; own flip).
#     FC: single-cell wide funcs (flat 106 / nested chain 112) — internal breaks.
#     FD: single-cell func fact flat 86..90 — the lone-cell boundary for funcs.
#     FE: wide func fact + siblings — fill layout of a squeezed func cell.
#   probeC.spthy (QuoteShapeProbe) — task 2: multi-quote corrections.
#     QA: [Faa 45, Qq('a..','b..') s] s=41..49 (two-quote fact own/occ).
#     QB: [Faa 45, Qm('a..', $ba) s] s=41..47 (one quote among 2 args).
#     QC: [Faa 45, Qt(<'a..', $ba, $bb>) s] s=38..46 (quote inside tuple-fact).
#   probeD.spthy (UnionShapeProbe) — task 3: ++-union display/breaks (multiset).
#     UA: single-cell wide unions (13/15/20 elems) — display format + breaks.
#     UB: single-cell union fact near the 87 boundary (one stretch-quote elem).
#     UC: unions nested in tuple / function argument.
#     UD: wide union fact + sibling — fill at squeezed budgets.
#     UE: [Faa 45, Un(union) s~39..46] — union-fact occupancy via X's flip.
#     DN: deep right-nested function chain, alone and squeezed.
import sys

def pvar(i, pref=""):
    a = "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
    return "$" + pref + a[i // 26] + a[i % 26]

def pvars(n, off=0):
    return [pvar(i + off) for i in range(n)]

def argfact(name, n, off=0):
    return f"{name}({', '.join(pvars(n, off))})"

def sib(p, idx, name="Sib"):
    return f"{name}('{'a' * p}{idx}')"

def in_prem(k, off=100):
    return f"In(<{', '.join(pvars(k, off))}>)"

class Battery:
    def __init__(self, thy, builtins=None, functions=None):
        self.thy = thy
        self.builtins = builtins
        self.functions = functions
        self.rules, self.lemmas, self.names = [], [], []
        self.n = 0

    def emit(self, name, prems, concls, comment):
        self.rules.append(
            f"// {comment}\n"
            f"rule {name}:\n  [ {', '.join(prems)} ]\n  --[ F{self.n}() ]->\n  [ {', '.join(concls)} ]\n")
        self.lemmas.append(f"lemma l_{name}:\n  exists-trace \"Ex #i. F{self.n}() @ #i\"\n")
        self.names.append(name)
        self.n += 1

    def write(self, path):
        with open(path, "w") as f:
            f.write(f"theory {self.thy}\nbegin\n\n")
            if self.builtins:
                f.write(f"builtins: {self.builtins}\n\n")
            if self.functions:
                f.write(f"functions: {self.functions}\n\n")
            for r in self.rules:
                f.write(r + "\n")
            for l in self.lemmas:
                f.write(l + "\n")
            f.write("end\n")
        with open(path.replace(".spthy", ".names"), "w") as f:
            for c in self.names:
                f.write(c + "\n")
        print(f"wrote {path}: {len(self.rules)} rules")

# ---------------- Battery A: audit redo (sqa placement/magnitude) ----------------
A = Battery("AuditSqaProbe")
for s in range(40, 49):                      # RA: base Faa(8)=45
    A.emit(f"RA_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8), sib(s - 11, f"{A.n:02d}")],
           f"RA: concl [Faa 45, Sib {s}] — X flips at s=42+k, sib at s=42+d")
for s in range(39, 47):                      # RB: base Faaa(8)=46
    A.emit(f"RB_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faaa", 8), sib(s - 11, f"{A.n:02d}")],
           f"RB: concl [Faaa 46, Sib {s}] — X flips at s=41+k, sib at s=41+d")
for s in (42, 44):                           # RC: order swap
    A.emit(f"RC_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [sib(s - 11, f"{A.n:02d}"), argfact("Faa", 8)],
           f"RC: concl [Sib {s}, Faa 45] — order-swapped RA")
for s in range(30, 36):                      # RD: triple, argfacts F(5)=H(5)=28
    A.emit(f"RD_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("F", 5), argfact("H", 5, off=5), sib(s - 11, f"{A.n:02d}")],
           f"RD: concl [F 28, H 28, Sib {s}] — X flips at s=31+k, sib at s=31+d")
for s in range(20, 25):                      # RE: floor (sib budget 87-70=17 -> 20)
    A.emit(f"RE_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Wff", 13), sib(s - 11, f"{A.n:02d}")],
           f"RE: concl [Wff 70, Sib {s}] — sib at floor 20: wraps at s=21+d_floor")
A.write("probeA.spthy")

# ---------------- Battery B: function-node shapes ----------------
fn_decls = []
for ln in range(1, 9):
    fn_decls.append(f"{'u' * ln}/7")         # u..uuuuuuuu arity 7 (FB 41..48)
for ln in range(3, 6):
    fn_decls.append(f"{'v' * ln}/6")         # vvv..vvvvv arity 6 (FB 38..40)
for ln in range(1, 6):
    fn_decls.append(f"{'w' * ln}/16")        # w..wwwww arity 16 (FD 86..90)
fn_decls += ["q/20", "p/2"]
B = Battery("FuncShapeProbe", functions=", ".join(fn_decls))

def funcfact(fname, k, off=0, outer="Q"):
    return f"{outer}({fname}({', '.join(pvars(k, off))}))"

for s in range(38, 41):                      # FB: arity-6, name len s-35
    B.emit(f"FB_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8), funcfact("v" * (s - 35), 6, off=8)],
           f"FB: concl [Faa 45, Q(func) {s}] — func occupancy via X flip at C>42")
for s in range(41, 49):                      # FB: arity-7, name len s-40
    B.emit(f"FB_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8), funcfact("u" * (s - 40), 7, off=8)],
           f"FB: concl [Faa 45, Q(func) {s}]")
B.emit("FC_1", [in_prem(2)], [funcfact("q", 20)],
       "FC: lone Q(q(20 args)) flat 106 — does the reference break inside q(...)?")
chain = pvar(13)
for i in range(12, -1, -1):
    chain = f"p({pvar(i)}, {chain})"
B.emit("FC_3", [in_prem(2)], [f"Q({chain})"],
       "FC: lone Q(p($aa, p($ab, ...))) deep chain — internal break shape")
for ln in range(1, 6):                       # FD: lone func fact flat 85+len
    B.emit(f"FD_{85 + ln}", [in_prem(2)], [funcfact("w" * ln, 16)],
           f"FD: lone Q({'w' * ln}(16 args)) flat {85 + ln} — lone-cell boundary")
B.emit("FE_1", [in_prem(2)], [funcfact("q", 20), sib(19, f"{B.n:02d}")],
       "FE: [Q(q(20)) 106, Sib 30] — squeezed func fill")
B.emit("FE_2", [in_prem(2)], [funcfact("q", 20), argfact("Faa", 8, off=20)],
       "FE: [Q(q(20)) 106, Faa 45] — squeezed func fill vs argfact")
B.write("probeB.spthy")

# ---------------- Battery C: quote shapes ----------------
C = Battery("QuoteShapeProbe")
for s in range(41, 50):                      # QA: Qq('a'*q1+i, 'b'*4+i), s=q1+q2+10
    q1 = s - 10 - 6                          # q2 content = 4+2 digits = 6 chars
    C.emit(f"QA_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8),
            f"Qq('{'a' * q1}{C.n:02d}', '{'b' * 4}{C.n:02d}')"],
           f"QA: concl [Faa 45, Qq 2-quote {s}] — own flip 43+d2, occ flip 42+k2")
for s in range(41, 48):                      # QB: Qm('a'*q+i, $ba), s=q+2+13
    q = s - 15                               # flat = 2+2+(q+2+2)+2+3+2 = q+15
    C.emit(f"QB_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8), f"Qm('{'a' * q}{C.n:02d}', {pvar(8)})"],
           f"QB: concl [Faa 45, Qm quote+var {s}]")
for s in range(38, 47):                      # QC: Qt(<'a'*q+i, $ba, $bb>), s=q+22
    q = s - 22                               # flat = 2+2+(1+q+2+2+2+3+2+3+1)+2
    C.emit(f"QC_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8), f"Qt(<'{'a' * q}{C.n:02d}', {pvar(8)}, {pvar(9)}>)"],
           f"QC: concl [Faa 45, Qt tuple-with-quote {s}] — quote-in-tuple corrections")
C.write("probeC.spthy")

# ---------------- Battery D: ++-unions + deep nesting ----------------
D = Battery("UnionShapeProbe", builtins="multiset")

def union(elems):
    return " ++ ".join(elems)

for n in (13, 15, 20):                       # UA: lone wide unions
    D.emit(f"UA_{n}", [in_prem(2)], [f"U({union(pvars(n))})"],
           f"UA: lone U({n}-elem union) — display format + break shape")
for q in range(37, 42):                      # UB: 8 vars + stretch quote
    D.emit(f"UB_{q}", [in_prem(2)],
           [f"U({union(pvars(8) + [chr(39) + 'z' * q + f'{D.n:02d}' + chr(39)])})"],
           f"UB: lone union with quote {q} — boundary sweep")
D.emit("UC_1", [in_prem(2)],
       [f"V(<{union(pvars(6))}, {pvar(30)}, '{'c' * 30}x1'>)"],
       "UC: union inside a tuple arg")
D.emit("UC_2", [in_prem(2)],
       [f"W(qq({union(pvars(14))}), {pvar(30)})"],
       "UC: union inside a function argument")
D.emit("UD_1", [in_prem(2)], [f"U({union(pvars(20))})", sib(19, f"{D.n:02d}")],
       "UD: [U(20-union), Sib 30] — squeezed union fill")
D.emit("UD_2", [in_prem(2)], [f"U({union(pvars(20))})", argfact("Faa", 8, off=20)],
       "UD: [U(20-union), Faa 45] — squeezed union fill vs argfact")
for s in range(39, 47):                      # UE: union sib occupancy
    # assume display U( x++y++... ) unknown; use 5 vars + stretch quote to step
    q = s - 34                               # guess flat = q+34 if unspaced-parenned
    D.emit(f"UE_{s:02d}", [in_prem(2)],
           [argfact("Faa", 8),
            f"Un({union(pvars(5, 8) + [chr(39) + 'y' * q + f'{D.n:02d}' + chr(39)])})"],
           f"UE: concl [Faa 45, Un(union) ~{s}] — union occupancy via X flip")
D.emit("DN_1", [in_prem(2)], [f"Q({chain})", sib(19, f"{D.n:02d}")],
       "DN: [deep chain, Sib 30] — squeezed nested-func fill")
D.functions = "qq/14, p/2"
D.write("probeD.spthy")

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# OOM guard: prover is the preferred kill target, capped at 24GB address space
echo 1000 > /proc/self/oom_score_adj 2>/dev/null || true
ulimit -v 25165824 2>/dev/null || true
# Serve a .spthy in the HS interactive UI on a given port (--port= form).
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
BIN="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
case "$1" in
  start)
    d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/";
    nohup "$BIN" interactive "$d" --port="$3" >/tmp/r10srv_$3.log 2>&1 &
    echo $! > /tmp/r10srv_$3.pid; sleep 5; echo "up on $3 pid $(cat /tmp/r10srv_$3.pid)";;
  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/r10srv_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/r10srv_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
esac
EOF
